data.title: Chip, AI stocks mixed, while broader market shows positive sentiment
data.description:
Chip stocks were mixed on Friday despite the larger market seeing some gains after yesterday's decline.

AI chipmakers, Nvidia's (NVDA) stock was largely flat but in the red, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) declined around 1%. Broadcom (AVGO) dipped about 1%, but Qualcomm (QCOM) seemed to buck the trend and rose nearly 2%.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) climbed around 0.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) rose around 1%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) had advanced by about 1.2%.

Shares of GlobalFoundries (GFS) soared nearly 5%, Texas Instruments (TXN) surged about 4%, and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) jumped around 3%. Analog Devices (ADI) climbed nearly 2%, while Micron Technology (MU), Marvell Technology (MRVL) and Intel (INTC) rose about 1% each. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) fell nearly 1%.

Software makers Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir Technologies (PLTR) each dipped around 1%. However, Adobe (ADBE) rose about 4%.

Chip equipment makers: Applied Materials (AMAT) climbed about 2%, while Lam Research (LRCX) was also in the green. KLA (KLAC) and ASML (ASML) each fell nearly -2%.

On Thursday, a bipartisan group of U.S. lawmakers unveiled a bill in the House that would restrict the purchase of Chinese chipmaking equipment by CHIPS Act grant recipients for 10 years.

data.title: Post Holdings targets $1.50B-$1.54B adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2026 as cash flow and Foodservice growth drive outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Post Holdings (POST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert Vitale reported a strong close to fiscal 2025, highlighting the company's ability to navigate regulatory changes, tariffs, Avian Flu, and uncertain consumer sentiment. He stated, "our portfolio of businesses displayed resilient and delivered strong results" and emphasized expectations for continued volume growth in Foodservice, discipline in Retail, and a focus on cost reduction and profitable brand investments. Vitale noted, "a highlight of FY '25 was our strong operating cash flow, which allowed us to maintain flat net leverage, while making key capital investments completing 2 tactical acquisitions and buying back over 11% of the company."
  • Vitale announced expectations for a "meaningful increase in FY '26 free cash flow" due to reduced capital spending and benefits from the new tax law, stating the company can "be opportunistic with our capital allocation decisions."
  • COO Jeff Zadoks reported strong consolidated results in Q4, with cold chain businesses navigating HPAI effectively and cost reductions offsetting lower retail volumes. He noted, "Volumes for these higher-margin egg products grew nearly 9% in the quarter and approximately 6% for the full year."
  • CFO Matt Mainer stated, "Fourth quarter consolidated net sales were $2.2 billion and adjusted EBITDA was $425 million. Sales increased 12% driven by our acquisition of 8th Avenue."

Outlook

  • CFO Mainer provided fiscal 2026 guidance: "including 2 months of pasta contribution, we expect our FY '26 adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $1.50 billion to $1.54 billion. This range reflects approximately a 1% to 4% growth rate to a normalized FY '25."
  • Mainer also noted, "Relative to FY '25 Q4, we expect Q1 adjusted EBITDA to decrease meaningfully, driven by HPAI normalization and seasonality declines in U.S. and U.K. cereal, partially offset by seasonality benefits in Refrigerated Retail. For the full year, we expect second half favorability to the first half."
  • CapEx for FY '26 is projected at $350 million to $390 million, a reduction from FY '25 following major investments in PCB and Foodservice.

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q4 were $2.2 billion and adjusted EBITDA reached $425 million, with sales growth attributed to the 8th Avenue acquisition.
  • Excluding 8th Avenue, net sales declined due to lower pet food and cereal volumes, partially offset by Avian Influenza-driven pricing and egg volume growth.
  • Foodservice net sales increased 20% with an 11% volume increase; adjusted EBITDA for Foodservice was up 50% due to Avian Influenza-driven pricing and volume growth in value-added egg and potato products.
  • Refrigerated Retail net sales were flat, but adjusted EBITDA rose 44%, benefiting from Avian Influenza pricing adders and lower SG&A costs.
  • Weetabix net sales increased 4%, aided by currency tailwinds, while segment adjusted EBITDA increased 1%.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was approximately $150 million; full-year free cash flow was nearly $500 million. The company repurchased 2.6 million shares in Q4 and 6.4 million shares for fiscal 2025, with additional buybacks following the quarter.

Q&A

  • Andrew Lazar, Barclays: Asked whether M&A or share buybacks are more attractive given current market dynamics. CEO Vitale responded, "we don't necessarily differentiate between M&A and buybacks... what we try to do is compare them from a potential return perspective and a risk perspective."
  • Lazar, Barclays: Follow-up on cost reduction actions in the cereal segment. Vitale indicated further opportunities are being explored, particularly "line optimization rather than plant optimization."
  • Thomas Palmer, JPMorgan: Asked about normalized growth outlooks by segment. CFO Mainer stated PCB will be "more flat," while the rest of the portfolio is in line with algorithms.
  • Palmer, JPMorgan: Questioned Foodservice EBITDA run rate. Mainer maintained the $115 million benchmark, expected to grow to $120 million by year-end.
  • Matthew Smith, Stifel: Questioned Refrigerated Retail margins. Mainer indicated high teens margins are reasonable during holiday seasons, with slower periods returning to around 16%.
  • Smith, Stifel: Asked about private label trends. Vitale stated, "It's really price gap dependent."
  • Scott Marks, Jefferies: Inquired about innovation investments. COO Zadoks described upcoming line extensions in cereal and pet, including protein and granola products and a Nutrish relaunch.
  • Marks, Jefferies: Asked about Foodservice demand for value-added products. Zadoks described long-term industry shift toward higher value-added products and stickiness of liquid egg adoption.
  • Michael Lavery, Piper Sandler: Inquired about Pet business volume cadence and private label losses. Mainer detailed timing for lapping private label losses and volume expectations through fiscal year.
  • Lavery, Piper Sandler: Asked about cereal pricing and promotional activity. Vitale described increased competitive pressure and changing promotions.
  • Marc Torrente, Wells Fargo: Questioned 8th Avenue and Pasta business contributions. Mainer confirmed no changes to 8th Avenue outlook and provided Pasta business contribution details.
  • Torrente, Wells Fargo: Asked about grocery volumes and SNAP impacts. Mainer characterized outlook as conservative, expecting marginal category improvement.
  • John Baumgartner, Mizuho: Asked about strategy on value versus premium product focus. Vitale said the portfolio is "built around choice," with innovation direction influenced by consumer trends.
  • Baumgartner, Mizuho: Asked about investment in side dishes. Zadoks outlined balancing branded and private label capacity to capture attractive opportunities.
  • Carla Casella, JPMorgan: Asked about the M&A environment. Vitale noted reluctance to transact at current multiples and reiterated capital allocation discipline. Casella also asked about refinancing, and Mainer said the company continues to monitor opportunities.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on structural versus cyclical category challenges, capital allocation, and volume outlook, indicating a slightly negative to cautious tone, especially regarding category declines and ongoing volume pressures.
  • Management maintained a confident and pragmatic tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience, cost control, and strategic flexibility. During Q&A, management remained disciplined and provided detailed responses, occasionally displaying caution in outlook language, as when Vitale discussed cost reductions: "the magnitude start to get smaller as the bigger things like plant closure have occurred."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tones remain consistently cautious, with analysts continuing to probe volume and margin headwinds and management reiterating their focus on cost control, innovation, and disciplined capital allocation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fiscal 2026 guidance for adjusted EBITDA is now explicitly set at $1.50 billion to $1.54 billion, compared to the prior range of $1.5 billion to $1.52 billion in Q3, signaling slightly higher expectations.
  • Management continues to focus on cost reduction, capital discipline, and balancing M&A with share repurchases, similar to the previous quarter.
  • Analysts' focus shifted slightly from leadership changes and integration of recent acquisitions in Q3 to ongoing volume pressures, capital allocation, and innovation in Q4.
  • Management's confidence remains steady, but their language continues to reflect caution regarding category headwinds and the timing of volume recoveries, especially in Cereal and Pet.
  • Key metrics such as free cash flow and buybacks increased sequentially, while leverage remained flat.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted uncertainty from regulatory changes, tariffs, Avian Flu, and consumer sentiment as ongoing risks.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about persistent volume declines in core brands and the pace of recovery in Pet and Cereal categories.
  • Management is pursuing cost reductions, innovation, and portfolio optimization as mitigation strategies, while maintaining flexibility in capital allocation.

Final Takeaway

Post Holdings closed fiscal 2025 with robust operating cash flow and solid segment performance despite ongoing volume challenges in Cereal and Pet. Management projects adjusted EBITDA of $1.50 billion to $1.54 billion for fiscal 2026 and expects free cash flow to rise with lower capital spending and tax benefits. The company will continue disciplined capital allocation, weighing M&A against share buybacks, and focus on innovation and margin protection across a diversified portfolio as volume headwinds persist in key categories.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Axalta holder pushes for restructured AkzoNobel deal - report
data.description:
Axalta Coatings (AXTA) investor Shapiro Capital Management wants the company to restructure its announced deal with AkzoNobel (OTCQX:AKZOF).

"We are very disappointed," Shapiro Capital Management President Louis Shapiro said in an interview with the WSJ  on Friday. "Axalta shareholders should get the premium they deserve."

Shapiro is threatening to vote against the combination unless the deal is restructured. He wants Axalta's board to seek better terms or consider alternative options to a merger with Akzo. Shapiro Capital had a 1.1% stake in Axalta as of Sept. 30.

The comments come after Axalta shareholder Artisan Partners also came out against  the deal earlier this week.

Axalta (AXTA) on Tuesday announced an all-stock merger with AkzoNobel (OTCQX:AKZOF) to create a global coatings company with roughly $17 billion in pro-forma revenue and $3.3 billion in EBITDA, supported by an estimated $600 million in cost synergies.

Shares of Axalta (AXTA) rose 5.5% on Friday.

data.title: Wayfair well-positioned for share gains and top-line growth in 2026 -- Truist
data.description:
Wayfair (W) is well-positioned for 2026 thanks to a cleaned up balance sheet, CEO incentive plan tied to higher stock price targets, and the re-platforming that is starting to yield “tangible benefits,” said Truist’s Youssef Squali, all of which set up the home furnishings retailer to sustain share gains and improve top-line growth.

Despite an uncertain macro weighing on the home furnishings' category, Squali is confident that Buy-rated Wayfair (W) can achieve 30% gross and 10% adjusted EBITDA margins, albeit predicated on a normalized environment. While Wayfair (W) is unlikely to see an AEBITDA margin in the mid-30% range until the background normalizes, the company is achieving leverage through strict cost discipline in its operating expense base along with a “maniacal” focus on the incrementality of each dollar invested in the platform.

The company is also working with large language models to capitalize on the rise of agentic commerce. As recently as last week, Google (GOOG) launched an agentic checkout feature with Wayfair (W) as an initial partner.

“While traffic from these AI sources remains very small today, we believe it will become critically important to growth over time as adoption from consumers grows,” Squali says. Crucially, Wayfair’s (W) unique inventory stands to benefit disproportionately from the rise of agentic AI versus other e-commerce platforms.

Squali’s endorsement of Wayfair (W) follows a better-than-expected third quarter that included a tripling of its quarterly profit, increased spending per customer, and higher number of delivered orders, all of which seemed to defy a “stubbornly sluggish category,” said CEO Niraj Shah on the company’s earnings call.

“It is important to note that our plan to grow is driven by Wayfair-specific factors and is not reliant upon a recovery in the housing market,” Shah notes.

As far as the lingering risk from tariffs, Wayfair (W) remains fluid, but the company’s marketplace dynamics provide it with insulation from a material adverse impact. These dynamics include Wayfair’s (W) success in incentivizing suppliers to keep prices low, a diverse supplier base, and the “power of the substitution effect” on the platform.  

Along with reiterating a Buy rating on Wayfair (W), Squali maintains a $120 price target, which implies 23% upside from Thursday’s closing price.

Wayfair (W) shares last traded 9% higher.

data.title: Trump aims to have Obamacare alternative settled by Jan. 30
data.description:
President Trump on Sunday said he hopes that an alternative to the Affordable Care Act, colloquially known as Obamacare, will be developed by Jan. 30, 2026.

The signature element of that alternative, which the president mentioned earlier in November, will be to replace the subsidies that currently go to health insurers with those funds instead going into accounts for individuals to buy health insurance.

Appearing on "The Brian Kilmeade Show" on Fox News Radio Friday morning, Trump called the ACA the "Unaffordable Care Act," adding that with his plan, "you'd get better health insurance and save a lot of money."

The president noted that he believes he has bipartisan support for his plan, adding that Republicans Sens. Katie Britt (Ala.) and Rick Scott (Fla.) are spearheading the effort.

One of the key sticking points of the recently ended federal government shutdown was the insistence by Democrats that ACA subsidies as they are currently remain for 2026, instead of lowering them to pre COVID-19 pandemic levels as Republicans support. If the subsidies go down, premiums for individuals on health insurance exchanges are set to rise dramatically. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: 'In the near term': Four words has Wall Street rebounding more than 1%
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday bounced back strongly from its stunning reversal in the previous session, largely led by a rebound in December interest rate cut odds thanks to some dovish comments from New York Fed President John Williams.

All three major averages jumped above 1% each. The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last +1.3%, the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +1.5%. The Nasdaq on Thursday had initially gained more than 2%, only to end the day sliding more than 2%.  

Four words did the trick on Friday.

"I view monetary policy as being modestly restrictive, although somewhat less so than before our recent actions. Therefore, I still see room for a further adjustment in the near term to the target range for the federal funds rate to move the stance of policy closer to the range of neutral, thereby maintaining the balance between the achievement of our two goals," Williams said in prepared remarks on Friday at a Central Bank of Chile conference.

December rate cut odds surged to 71.5%, according to the CME FedWatch tool, from 39.1% a day ago. 

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the three major averages: (DIA), (DDM), (UDOW), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QLD), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Novamax rises after a seven-day slide
data.description:
Novavax (NVAX) rose 4.98% to $6.53 in afternoon trading on Friday, snapping a seven-session losing streak, with notable drops of 3.38% and 3.50% on November 18 and 19.

Novavax now expects to reach profitability in 2028, with the timing tied to upcoming vaccine launches under its Sanofi partnership.

The company also shared updated revenue outlook, noting higher guidance for next year even as its latest results showed a wider quarterly loss. It is hoping that the upcoming COVID–flu combination shot will help strengthen its outlook, but demand for its current COVID vaccine has stayed soft.

At the same time, one of its major shareholders has been pressing for broader changes. The investor has raised concerns about the slow uptake of the company’s vaccine and said Novavax has not been able to turn its science into stronger commercial results, adding pressure on the board to review its options.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, NVAX is rated Hold with a score of 2.81 out of 5, highlighted by A+ in valuation, B- in profitability, but weaker growth with a C- rating, D+ in momentum, and F in revisions.

SA analysts also recommend holding the stock.

While on the Wall Street, five of nine analysts rate the shares Strong Buy, while one advises Hold and three rate Sell or lower.

Despite today’s rebound, shares remain down by 18.78% YTD and have lost around 24% in the past month.

data.title: Home Depot set to snap seven straight sessions of losses
data.description:
Home Depot (HD) shares set to snap seven straight sessions of losses, as the stock gained more than 3.5% during afternoon trading on Friday.

However, the firm’s shares slipped about 11.5% in the preceding seven sessions.

HD is down 11.7% over the past month. The stock closed 0.63% lower on Thursday at $332.38.

Earlier this week, the company reported mixed Q3 earnings as its Non-GAAP EPS of $3.74 missed the consensus estimate of $3.83 by $0.09, while its revenue increased 2.8% Y/Y to $41.35 billion.

Seeking Alpha analyst YR Research highlighted that Home Depot (HD) missed estimates once again, as a highly anticipated market recovery continues to be pushed out.

"Perhaps the guidance cut will finally be the driver that realigns market expectations with a pressured consumer reality, as Home Depot now expects comparable sales to grow less than 1%," advised YR Research.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating, HD has a Hold rating with a score of 3.08 out of 5. The company received an A+ for profitability and a D- for valuation and growth factors.

Seeking Alpha analysts are also cautious and see the stock as a Hold.

Turning to the Wall Street community, 23 out of 37 analysts rated HD a Buy and above, 14 analysts gave the stock a Hold recommendation, and no one rated it a Sell or below.

Overall, the stock has declined more than 13% so far this year, compared to a nearly 12% rise in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Google exec says compute needs to be doubled every 6 months to meet demand: report
data.description:
An executive working on artificial intelligence infrastructure at Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) recently told employees the company needs to double its compute capacity every six months to meet demand, CNBC reported.

Amin Vahdat, a vice president at Google Cloud, gave a presentation to employees that included a slide on compute capacity, which read “Now we must double every 6 months.... the next 1000x in 4–5 years,” the news outlet reported, citing the presentation. The comments were made at a meeting on Nov. 6. 

“The competition in AI infrastructure is the most critical and also the most expensive part of the AI race,” Vahdat said during the meeting.

The search giant needs to “be able to deliver 1,000 times more capability, compute, storage networking for essentially the same cost and increasingly, the same power, the same energy level,” Vahdat added. “It won’t be easy, but through collaboration and co-design, we’re going to get there.”

A Google spokesperson told Seeking Alpha that the claim was “misleading.” 

"Amin was not talking about a capital buildout of anything approaching the magnitude suggested”" the spokesperson said via email. “In reality, he simply noted that demand for AI services means we are being asked to provide significantly more computing capacity, which we are driving through efficiency across hardware, software, and model optimizations, in addition to new investments. For example, our new Ironwood pods offer a 10x peak performance improvement than their predecessors released just a year ago.”  

Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai and CFO Anat Ashkenazi were also at the meeting and took questions.

One of the questions Pichai took from an employee was about the potential bursting of the AI bubble. Pichai acknowledged the fact that the question is now in the “zeitgeist,” but reiterated his stance that the risk of underinvesting is high. 

“I think it’s always difficult during these moments because the risk of underinvesting is pretty high,” Pichai said, according to CNBC. “I actually think for how extraordinary the cloud numbers were, those numbers would have been much better if we had more compute.” 

The report comes one day after tech giant Nvidia (NVDA) reported better-than-expected results and guidance. Shares initially rose after the figures were released, and management had a response for several investor concerns. However, the gains evaporated on Thursday amid continued worries about the bursting of the AI bubble.

Alphabet is one of Nvidia's customers and also uses its own tensor processing units for various applications and services.

(This story has been updated to reflect Google's response.)

data.title: International Land Alliance secures up to $50M in institutional financing
data.description:
• International Land Alliance (ILAL) on Friday said it has secured an up to $50 million financing facility from an institutional investor.
  • The company intends to use the net proceeds to acquire 300 acres of land and structures located adjacent to the its Rancho Costa Verde development in San Felipe, Mexico.
  • Simultaneously, ILAL entered into an agreement to provide real estate services for the properties of such investor.
  • The company intends to use a portion of the initial proceeds from the financing facility to support acquisitions, joint ventures, project expansions, and serve as a strategic reserve to support long-term growth opportunities.
  • ILAL is +6.32% to $0.1699.
  • Source: Press Release
